verses.text: And Solomon determined to build an house for the name of the LORD, and an house for his kingdom. And Solomon told out threescore and ten thousand men to bear burdens, and fourscore thousand to hew in the mountain, and three thousand and six hundred to oversee them. And Solomon sent to Huram the king of Tyre, saying, As thou didst deal with David my father, and didst send him cedars to build him an house to dwell therein, even so deal with me. Behold, I build an house to the name of the LORD my God, to dedicate it to him, and to burn before him sweet incense, and for the continual shewbread, and for the burnt offerings morning and evening, on the sabbaths, and on the new moons, and on the solemn feasts of the LORD our God. This is an ordinance for ever to Israel. And the house which I build is great: for great is our God above all gods. But who is able to build him an house, seeing the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain him? who am I then, that I should build him an house, save only to burn sacrifice before him? Guidance: Solomon sets about building the temple, and his message to Huram of Tyre reveals a heart that grasps both the greatness of God and the true purpose of the house. He states plainly what the temple is for: 'to dedicate it to him, and to burn before him sweet incense, and for the continual shewbread, and for the burnt offerings morning and evening' — it is a house of worship and sacrifice. And then he soars into glorious theology: 'the house which I build is great: for great is our God above all gods. But who is able to build him an house, seeing the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain him? who am I then, that I should build him an house, save only to burn sacrifice before him?' The chapter is showing us the right understanding of God and of worship. The chapter is teaching us the transcendence of God — the infinite One whom 'the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain.' No building, however magnificent, could ever house the LORD; He fills heaven and earth and is greater than all. The chapter is teaching us, therefore, a deep humility: 'who am I then, that I should build him an house?' Solomon, the richest and wisest of kings, is awed and humbled before the greatness of God. The chapter is teaching us the true purpose of the temple — not to contain God, as if He needed a dwelling, but to be a place of sacrifice and worship, where His people might draw near to burn incense and offer their offerings. The chapter is teaching us that worship is for the great God who is great 'above all gods,' and that the magnificence of the house was meant to reflect, however faintly, the surpassing greatness of the One worshiped there. The chapter is teaching us to hold these two truths together: God is infinitely great, beyond all our works and worthy of our best; and yet He graciously condescends to meet His people in worship and to receive the sacrifices of humble hearts. Let our worship be both reverent before His greatness and humble in the knowledge that He needs nothing from us, save only the offering of grateful, adoring hearts.

The chapter is teaching us that worship is for the great God who is great 'above all gods,' and that the magnificence of the house was meant to reflect, however faintly, the surpassing greatness of the One worshiped there. The chapter is teaching us to hold these two truths together: God is infinitely great, beyond all our works and worthy of our best; and yet He graciously condescends to meet His people in worship and to receive the sacrifices of humble hearts. Let our worship be both reverent before His greatness and humble in the knowledge that He needs nothing from us, save only the offering of grateful, adoring hearts.

Send me now therefore a man cunning to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass, and in iron, and in purple, and crimson, and blue, and that can skill to grave with the cunning men that are with me in Judah and in Jerusalem, whom David my father did provide. Send me also cedar trees, fir trees, and algum trees, out of Lebanon: for I know that thy servants can skill to cut timber in Lebanon; and, behold, my servants shall be with thy servants, Even to prepare me timber in abundance: for the house which I am about to build shall be wonderful great. And, behold, I will give to thy servants, the hewers that cut timber, twenty thousand measures of beaten wheat, and twenty thousand measures of barley, and twenty thousand baths of wine, and twenty thousand baths of oil. Guidance: Solomon asks Huram for a master craftsman skilled in gold, silver, brass, iron, and rich fabrics and engraving, and for the finest timber of Lebanon, promising generous provision for the workers — 'for the house which I am about to build shall be wonderful great.' The chapter is showing us Solomon's determination that nothing but the best skill and the finest materials should go into the house of God. The chapter is teaching us that the worship and house of God deserve excellence — skilled workmanship, choice materials, the best that can be found. Solomon did not want shoddy or cheap work for the LORD's temple, but sought out the most cunning craftsmen and the most precious timber. We are taught that whatever we offer or build for God should be done with care, skill, and our best resources, for He is worthy of excellence. The chapter is teaching us the value of skilled labour devoted to God; the craftsman who works in gold and engraving, the woodcutter skilled in felling cedar, all have their place in building the house of the LORD. Honest skill, when offered to God, is a holy thing. The chapter is teaching us, too, the fairness and generosity of Solomon toward the workers: he promises abundant wheat, barley, wine, and oil to Huram's servants. Those who labour, even foreign labourers, were to be well provided for; the work of God was not to be built on the backs of the unpaid or the cheated. The chapter is teaching us that good work deserves good wages, and that generosity and fairness toward workers honour the God whose house they build. The chapter is teaching us to bring excellence, skill, and our best materials to the service of God, and to deal justly and generously with all who labour — for the God who is 'wonderful great' is worthy of a wonderful work, done well and done right.

Then Huram the king of Tyre answered in writing, which he sent to Solomon, Because the LORD hath loved his people, he hath made thee king over them. Huram said moreover, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth, who hath given to David the king a wise son, endued with prudence and understanding, that might build an house for the LORD, and an house for his kingdom. And now I have sent a cunning man, endued with understanding, of Huram my father's, The son of a woman of the daughters of Dan, and his father was a man of Tyre, skilful to work in gold, and in silver, in brass, in iron, in stone, and in timber, in purple, in blue, and in fine linen, and in crimson; also to grave any manner of graving, and to find out every device which shall be put to him, with thy cunning men, and with the cunning men of my lord David thy father. Now therefore the wheat, and the barley, the oil, and the wine, which my lord hath spoken of, let him send unto his servants: And we will cut wood out of Lebanon, as much as thou shalt need: and we will bring it to thee in floats by sea to Joppa; and thou shalt carry it up to Jerusalem. Guidance: Huram, the pagan king of Tyre, answers in words that surprise us with their piety: 'Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth, who hath given to David the king a wise son.' He acknowledges that 'the LORD hath loved his people' and made Solomon king over them. And he sends a master craftsman — Huram-abi, the son of a woman of Dan and a Tyrian father — skilled in every kind of work. The chapter is showing us a Gentile king blessing the true God, the Maker of heaven and earth, and gladly contributing to the building of His house. The chapter is teaching us that the knowledge of the true God was not confined to Israel; even a foreign king could confess that the LORD made heaven and earth and loves His people — a foreshadowing of the day when the nations would come to the light of Israel's God. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD draws even outsiders to honour His name and serve His purposes; Huram's cedars and his craftsman helped build the temple, as the resources of the nations were laid at the service of God's house. The chapter is teaching us, in Huram-abi the craftsman of mixed heritage — a Danite mother and a Tyrian father — that the LORD uses people of every background and lineage in His work; like Bezaleel who built the tabernacle, this skilled man devoted his gifts to the house of God, and his foreign blood was no barrier to sacred service. The chapter is teaching us that skill is a gift of God to be employed for His glory, and that the LORD gathers workers and resources from far and wide for the building of His kingdom. The chapter is teaching us to recognize and welcome the good that comes even from unexpected quarters, and to see in Huram's confession and Huram-abi's craftsmanship a glimpse of the gospel's wide embrace — that God would be worshiped and served by people of every nation, and that the wealth and skill of the Gentiles would be brought into the house of the LORD.

The chapter is teaching us that skill is a gift of God to be employed for His glory, and that the LORD gathers workers and resources from far and wide for the building of His kingdom. The chapter is teaching us to recognize and welcome the good that comes even from unexpected quarters, and to see in Huram's confession and Huram-abi's craftsmanship a glimpse of the gospel's wide embrace — that God would be worshiped and served by people of every nation, and that the wealth and skill of the Gentiles would be brought into the house of the LORD.

Then Huram the king of Tyre answered in writing, which he sent to Solomon, Because the LORD hath loved his people, he hath made thee king over them. Huram said moreover, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth, who hath given to David the king a wise son, endued with prudence and understanding, that might build an house for the LORD, and an house for his kingdom. And now I have sent a cunning man, endued with understanding, of Huram my father's, The son of a woman of the daughters of Dan, and his father was a man of Tyre, skilful to work in gold, and in silver, in brass, in iron, in stone, and in timber, in purple, in blue, and in fine linen, and in crimson; also to grave any manner of graving, and to find out every device which shall be put to him, with thy cunning men, and with the cunning men of my lord David thy father. Now therefore the wheat, and the barley, the oil, and the wine, which my lord hath spoken of, let him send unto his servants: And we will cut wood out of Lebanon, as much as thou shalt need: and we will bring it to thee in floats by sea to Joppa; and thou shalt carry it up to Jerusalem. Guidance: Huram, the pagan king of Tyre, answers in words that surprise us with their piety: 'Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth, who hath given to David the king a wise son.' He acknowledges that 'the LORD hath loved his people' and made Solomon king over them. And he sends a master craftsman — Huram-abi, the son of a woman of Dan and a Tyrian father — skilled in every kind of work. The chapter is showing us a Gentile king blessing the true God, the Maker of heaven and earth, and gladly contributing to the building of His house. The chapter is teaching us that the knowledge of the true God was not confined to Israel; even a foreign king could confess that the LORD made heaven and earth and loves His people — a foreshadowing of the day when the nations would come to the light of Israel's God. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD draws even outsiders to honour His name and serve His purposes; Huram's cedars and his craftsman helped build the temple, as the resources of the nations were laid at the service of God's house. The chapter is teaching us, in Huram-abi the craftsman of mixed heritage — a Danite mother and a Tyrian father — that the LORD uses people of every background and lineage in His work; like Bezaleel who built the tabernacle, this skilled man devoted his gifts to the house of God, and his foreign blood was no barrier to sacred service. The chapter is teaching us that skill is a gift of God to be employed for His glory, and that the LORD gathers workers and resources from far and wide for the building of His kingdom. The chapter is teaching us to recognize and welcome the good that comes even from unexpected quarters, and to see in Huram's confession and Huram-abi's craftsmanship a glimpse of the gospel's wide embrace — that God would be worshiped and served by people of every nation, and that the wealth and skill of the Gentiles would be brought into the house of the LORD.

And Solomon numbered all the strangers that were in the land of Israel, after the numbering wherewith David his father had numbered them; and they were found an hundred and fifty thousand and three thousand and six hundred. And he set threescore and ten thousand of them to be bearers of burdens, and fourscore thousand to be hewers in the mountain, and three thousand and six hundred overseers to set the people a work. Guidance: Solomon organizes a vast labour force for the building — 153,600 strangers (resident aliens) set to bear burdens, hew stone in the mountain, and oversee the work. The great house of God would be built by the ordered labour of many thousands of hands. The chapter is showing us the immense, organized effort required to build the temple; a work so great demanded a great and well-marshaled workforce. The chapter is teaching us that the work of God often requires many labourers working together in good order — bearers, hewers, overseers — each with a part to play, all coordinated toward the one great end. No single pair of hands could build the temple; it took a multitude, rightly arranged. The chapter is teaching us the value of organization and shared labour in any great undertaking for God; Solomon did not leave the work to chance but numbered, assigned, and oversaw the labourers. The chapter is teaching us, too, that the LORD's house was built in part by the strangers within Israel — the resident foreigners — so that even the labour of the building drew in those from outside the covenant people, a quiet anticipation of how the church, the true temple, would be built up of people from every nation. The chapter is teaching us that great works for God are accomplished through the patient, ordered toil of many, and that there is a place and a task for all — the burden-bearer, the stonecutter, the overseer — in the building of the house of the LORD. Let us be willing to take our place, however humble, among the many hands that build for God, knowing that the grandest house of worship rises only through the faithful labour of the many, each doing his appointed part.
